paragraphs.body: Dear, Jamaica Kincad ​ Good evening Jamaica I hope this letter finds you well. My name is Scarlett, I'm a student athlete that attends University of Pittsburgh. I’m very fascinated by your essay “Girl,” which talked about some of the social standards that are expected of women in your culture and how it can feel overwhelming and unbearable. Also I appreciated some of the deeper topics you’ve covered over that may have been missed by the masses. To me the framework of the essay was meant to make the reader feel how you felt making the essay mad, frustrated, and in need of space. As a male I don’t feel as I fully could comprehend how you truly felt because it isn’t something I could ever experience however I still felt a sort of relation conveyed by your strong emotions towards the gender expectations in your culture. What I can relate to ,though, as a black man in America is constant pressure and expectations for you to be a certain way and act a certain way or else you will be labeled and put in categories that don’t really define who you are. But I love how your essay was meant to break the grammar rules and I took that as a metaphor to break the societal rules meant to limit you and limit what you can do. What I am curious about and have no been able to figure out what your mention of Sunday throughout the essay I originally thought of the connection between Sunday and the day of worship and I thought it’s how usually on a day of “rest” it’s ironic because that is when they have you doing the most work, but now I find myself second guessing that thought and really wanted to know what you intended for the reader to feel.

annotations.text: What I am curious about and have no been able to figure out what your mention of Sunday throughout the essay I originally thought of the connection between Sunday and the day of worship and I thought it’s how usually on a day of “rest” it’s ironic because that is when they have you doing the most work,
annotations.comment: That is a very astute point! This is a great question to ask--it covers so much cultural understanding and personal practice--nice!

annotations.text: I could ever experience however I still felt a sort of relation conveyed by your strong emotions towards the gender expectations in your culture. What I can relate to ,though, as a black man in America is constant pressure and expectations for you to be a certain way and act a certain way or else you will be labeled and put in categories that don’t really define who you are.
annotations.comment: I feel like this is the heart of your letter--could you describe an experience that you have had personally that might relate to what Kincaid has written? This would allow her to feel the connection for herself.

You're doing a great job of engaging the author in this letter. I really would love to see you bring a little more of yourself, your own experiences and observations to the letter. Maybe a brief anecdote or a cultural observation would really help bring your point home. Keep it up!
